strengths: Clear identification of client and scenario with multiple evidence points
Relevant consultation shown through screenshots and quotations
Appropriate, measurable success criteria aligned to client needs
weaknesses: Consultation depth could be deeper to fully satisfy highest band
Rationale lacks comparison of multiple technical solutions
Success criteria omit some system considerations (e.g. platform compatibility)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place Planning solidly in the 3â4 band. The scenario and client investigation go beyond a single statement but are not exhaustive. The rationale is relevant but not fully justified through comparative analysis. Success criteria are measurable yet not comprehensive. A mark of 4/6 accurately reflects these strengths and areas for improvement.

strengths: Integration of timers, persistence via SQLite, and conditional UI flows shows moderate complexity
Effective use of Android Studio tools, XML layouts, and emulator profiling
Comprehensive source attribution and inline comments explaining key techniques
weaknesses: No automated testing tools or version control evidence
Some source explanations are brief and missing licensing details for assets
Algorithms are functional but not highly advanced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Development is clearly in the mid-band (5â8). All three strands demonstrate appropriate tool use, source documentation, and a moderate level of ingenuity. An 8/12 acknowledges the solid integration and explanation while noting the absence of advanced algorithms and automated workflows.

strengths: Comprehensive GUI mock-ups and flowcharts conveying most navigation paths
Detailed database design and clear mapping of requirements to widgets and queries
Reasonably complete record of development tasks outlining the main sequence
weaknesses: Task log has gaps and lacks reflective commentary
Test plan missing boundary/exception cases and specific thresholds
Design overview omits low-level diagrams (e.g. class diagrams)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Solution Overview strands average between basic and top band performance. Task record and test plan fit the 3-4 band; the design overview approaches the top band but lacks some details. Awarding 4/6 reflects solid but not exhaustive planning of tasks, design, and testing.

strengths: All primary functions demonstrated and operate well in video
Minor glitches identified but core functionality meets client needs
Product structure allows for potential expansion with code duplication
weaknesses: Screen rotation bug resets timer, indicating instability under edge conditions
Extensibility is possible only through manual code changes and duplication
No evidence of plugin architecture or modular API design
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The product functions well overall (3â4 band) but exhibits flaws under rotation. Extensibility is acknowledged but difficult (1â2 band). A combined holistic mark of 3/4 balances solid functionality with limited extensibility.

strengths: Each success criterion revisited with evidence in evaluation table
Client questionnaire and peer feedback linked to usability insights
Recommendations are realistic and clearly tied to observed issues
weaknesses: Evaluations lack quantitative metrics and in-depth analysis
Feedback is limited in depth and does not show iterative dialogue
Recommendations lack feasibility details like time/cost estimates
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three evaluation strands sit squarely in the 3-4 band but are on the lower end due to brief analysis and lack of quantitative data. A mark of 3/6 reflects partial but not comprehensive evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student provides a hierarchical GUI mock-up, five detailed flowcharts covering every navigation path, an ER/table definition for the SQLite database and an explanation of how each functional requirement maps to widgets and SQL queries.  Links between components are clear and collectively convey a coherent conception of the whole product; only minor low-level details (e.g. class diagrams) are absent, placing it firmly in the top band but toward the lower mark.

The student explains that existing workout-tracking apps are too cluttered for the client and justifies choosing a minimalist timer + SQLite solution so the client can operate it quickly mid-session.  The explanation is relevant but stays general; it does not compare multiple alternatives or evaluate technical feasibility in depth, so a solid but not âfully justifiedâ rationale is evidenced.

Android Studio, XML layouts, Java/Kotlin code, and SQLiteOpenHelper are used effectively; screenshots of profiler output and emulator testing corroborate tool use.  However, no automated testing tools or version control evidence are shown, keeping the score moderate within the 5-8 band.

The testing plan lists seven meaningful test cases with method and expected result columns.  While each major function is addressed, timing values and acceptance thresholds are not always specific, and there is no reference to boundary or exception testing.  This depth aligns with a âbasic outlineâ rather than a fully detailed plan.

The student proposes three realistic improvementsâadding cloud backup, implementing input validation and refactoring code into MVCâwith concise justifications.  While sensible, they lack detailed feasibility or time estimates, so the recommendations are âlargely realisticâ rather than fully justified.

All on-line tutorials, icon sets and code snippets are listed in a bibliography, and inline comments cite sources.  Techniques such as intent passing and SQL CRUD operations are explained, but some explanations are brief (âstandard insert statementâ) and licence details for images are missing, warranting a lower-mid score in the 5-8 band.

A spreadsheet-style table logs 18 dated entries from âInitial interviewâ to âFinal usability testâ, each with task description and status.  Several gaps exist (e.g. no entries for two-week coding period) and reflections are missing, yet the record still outlines the main development sequence, satisfying the âpartially completeâ descriptor.

The application integrates multiple timers, dynamic SQLite storage, date-time stamping and conditional UI flows.  Although algorithms are not advanced, combining persistent data handling with responsive Android layouts shows a moderate level of complexity and some ingenuity (e.g. fetching only latest weight per exercise through a MAX(date) query).  This positioning fits mid-band (5-8).

Six success criteria are stated (accurate timers, editable weights, latest weight displayed, intuitive UI, etc.) and each is measurable.  However, coverage is not exhaustive (e.g. platform compatibility, data persistence longevity are omitted) and some criteria are vaguely worded ("very favourable usability rating").  Hence the list is appropriate but not comprehensive.

The appendix contains a signed client questionnaire and three bullet points of peer feedback.  Comments are directly linked to usability and minor bugs, but depth is limited (no iterative dialogue or reflection), meeting âsome feedbackâ requirements.

Because logic is tightly coupled to specific layout IDs, adding new exercise groups would require manual code duplication.  The student notes potential to âadd more exercises by copying blocksâ, indicating expansion is possible but cumbersome, corresponding to the middle band (1-2) with the higher mark awarded for acknowledging the issue.

The student identifies a real gym-goer as client and briefly explains the clientâs need to track weights, times and exercise form.  Screenshots of chat messages and quotations from two face-to-face meetings are included, showing that the student asked the client about desired functions and clarified priorities (e.g. âmust remember last weight usedâ).  While the consultation is not exhaustive, it is clearly more than a single statement, therefore meeting the descriptors for âevidence of consultationâ in the 3-4 band.

The demonstration video shows all primary functions working: timers count accurately, weights are saved and retrieved, and exercise descriptions display.  Minor glitches (screen rotation resets timer) are visible, so the product is functional but not flawless, matching the âproduct functions wellâ descriptor (3-4 marks) at the lower end.

Each of the six initial success criteria is revisited in a table stating whether the criterion was met and citing evidence (screenshots, timing comparisons).  Explanations are brief and do not reference quantitative measurements (e.g. usability rating values), so evaluation is partial rather than comprehensive.

annotations.comment: The introduction outlines intended functionalities but lacks connection to project timeline and task sequencing, which would contextualize the design phase more effectively.
The Break timer description is clear but could specify how durations are selected and validated (e.g., min/max limits, input controls) to ensure robust UI behavior.
Consider distinguishing between pause, stop, and reset states in the Chronometer description to clarify both user interactions and underlying state management.
The Exercise weight changer description effectively ties functionality to client needs, emphasizing persistent storage and automatic display of recent values.
Provide details on how exercise descriptions will be stored and retrieved (e.g., separate database table or JSON resource) to ensure consistency between UI and data layers.
The simplified flowchart effectively summarizes user navigation and key functions, providing a clear high-level overview for stakeholders.
Consider adding error or exception paths (e.g., timer not set, invalid input) to the simplified flowchart to capture edge cases and improve robustness.
The specific flowcharts for the Break and Chronometer buttons detail each decision clearly, supporting accurate implementation of state transitions.
The flowcharts lack explicit start/end notation and standard symbols; include these elements to improve readability and adhere to UML conventions.
The weight-selection flowchart on page 3 clearly maps navigation between muscle groups and exercises, showing moderate intricacy.
Include decision nodes for invalid or missing muscle-group data in the flowchart to account for error handling during weight selection.
The workout-data flowchart effectively shows data retrieval and display steps, guiding implementation of the core feature.
The weight-selection screen mockup lacks validation constraints and error messages; define numeric range checks and user feedback for invalid input.
Clarify how date-time values are formatted and localized on the Workout Data screen to ensure consistency across devices.
The flowchart does not handle the scenario where no workout data exists; add a conditional path to display an empty-state message.
The exercise-library flowcharts illustrate drill-down navigation clearly, aiding understanding of multi-screen interactions.
Consider annotating image and text rendering steps in the library flowchart to ensure designers include correct asset-loading logic.
Specify fixed dimensions or layout constraints for the Main Screen mockup (e.g., padding, margins) to support responsive UI development.
The Break timer screen mockup omits labels or indicators for dynamic countdown updates; clarify how users will see remaining time.
Include an explicit reset or clear control in the Chronometer screen mockup to indicate how users revert the timer to 00:00.
Suggest providing scroll or pagination behavior for the Select Workout list to accommodate longer exercise lists without overwhelming the UI.
The test plan covers main functions but lacks boundary and exception cases (e.g., zero or negative time inputs); include these to strengthen validation.
Add specific acceptance thresholds and measurement methods (e.g., Â±0.5s timer accuracy) to each test case to make results objectively verifiable.
The exercise description image lacks alt-text or asset metadata notation; include this information to support accessibility and consistency.
The database table design clearly specifies field names and types, aiding implementation of data persistence.
Consider using a TIMESTAMP or DATETIME type for workout_date instead of INTEGER to simplify querying and formatting date-time values.
